query:
You are a technical research assistant performing a deep, structured literature review of open-source GitHub repositories and self-hosted tooling. Your output must be comprehensive, citation-rich, and implementation-aware.

CONTEXT:
The user wants to convert a desktop computer into a personal remote powerhouse. It will be:
- Connected to a home router via ethernet (wired, static LAN IP or DHCP reservation)
- On constant power, treated as a low-cost home server
- Accessed remotely from a 2012 Apple MacBook (primary frontend/client endpoint)
- Also accessible wirelessly from a smartphone for filesystem browsing and triggering AI model jobs
- Running locally self-trained or fine-tuned ML models (e.g. Llama 3.1 8B) for remote inference jobs

RESEARCH DOMAINS — investigate each in depth:

1. SECURE REMOTE ACCESS LAYER
   - Open-source VPN and zero-config tunnel solutions: Tailscale, Headscale (self-hosted Tailscale control plane), WireGuard, ZeroTier, Cloudflare Tunnel (cloudflared), ngrok alternatives (frp, bore, rathole)
   - Dynamic DNS (DDNS) solutions compatible with home routers: ddclient, DuckDNS, No-IP
   - SSH hardening best practices for a home server exposed to the internet: fail2ban, key-only auth, non-standard port, UFW/iptables rules

2. WEB-BASED FRONTEND / FILE MANAGER UI
   - Open-source self-hosted web file managers: Filebrowser, Nextcloud, Seafile, Syncthing, CasaOS, Umbrel
   - Server dashboards with mobile-responsive UI: Cockpit, Heimdall, Homarr, Portainer, Dasherr
   - Evaluate: which GitHub repos are most actively maintained (stars, recent commits, open issues), which run well on a single desktop Linux machine with minimal RAM overhead

3. MACOS LEGACY CLIENT COMPATIBILITY (2012 MacBook)
   - macOS version likely capped at Monterey or older — what remote access clients and web UIs work on these OS versions
   - Best SSH clients for older macOS: native Terminal, mosh for unreliable connections
   - FUSE / SSHFS mounting for exposing remote filesystem as a local drive on macOS
   - macOS-compatible VPN clients for WireGuard and Tailscale on older OS versions

4. SMARTPHONE FILESYSTEM ACCESS
   - iOS and Android apps for SFTP, WebDAV, SMB, and NFS remote filesystem access: FE File Explorer, Solid Explorer, CX File Explorer, FileBrowser (app), Owlfiles
   - Open-source server-side solutions that serve WebDAV or SFTP natively: Filebrowser (built-in WebDAV), Nextcloud, SFTPGo
   - Triggering remote AI jobs from phone: lightweight web UI options, REST API trigger endpoints

5. LOCAL AI MODEL SERVING
   - Open-source inference servers for Llama-family models: Ollama, llama.cpp server, LM Studio (server mode), vLLM (for GPU), koboldcpp
   - GitHub repos: list URLs, star counts, last commit dates, notable forks
   - Job queue / async task systems that pair well with local model inference: Celery + Redis, RQ (Redis Queue), simple FastAPI + background tasks
   - How to expose a local model endpoint securely over the tunnel layer (reverse proxy: Caddy, Nginx, Traefik)

6. INTEGRATION ARCHITECTURE
   - How these layers compose: Tunnel → Reverse Proxy → Web UI + Model API
   - Docker Compose vs bare-metal install tradeoffs for a single-desktop setup
   - Recommended minimal stack for "get started today": what 3-5 tools cover 90% of the use case with least config friction

OUTPUT FORMAT:
- Section per domain above
- For each tool/repo: name, GitHub URL if applicable, brief description, pros/cons for this specific use case, compatibility notes for 2012 MacBook and smartphone access
- Conclude with a "Recommended Starter Stack" section: the minimal viable combo to install today, with rationale
- Flag any tools with known security issues or abandoned maintenance
result:

## Research Summary

**Duration:** 1346.9s | **Rounds:** 2 | **Queries:** 4 | **URLs Analyzed:** 13

sources.title: strongSwan - IPsec VPN for Linux, Android, FreeBSD, macOS, Windows

raw_findings.title: strongSwan - IPsec VPN for Linux, Android, FreeBSD, macOS, Windows
raw_findings.summary: StrongSwan supports IKEv2 and IPv6 IPsec tunnel connections, which can be used to set up a secure remote access layer.
